tokens: First heading out of here, John, is 030.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Shoot, that thing (possibly the DAC) drifts.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Charlie, can you turn that DAC off for another 19 minutes?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I'm going to be spending...(Stops to listen).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll give you a mark (when to turn the DAC on).
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I'll tell you, this ridge up here (north of the LM) would be a good place to park the Rover, Houston, if you want (to watch the launch from the north)....We're reading...I think it (meaning the Rover Nav system)'s working.
And that's how far away we are.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: This ridge would be a good place to park the Rover.
Up north of it (that is, north of the LM).
I don't know if you (meaning the LRV electronics package and associated radiators) can stand it thermally.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I don't think they can pan the camera like that, when we lift off.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're topping out of the little ridge.
We can now see Dome and Smoky.
On top of the ridge, there are boulders much like we seen yesterday.
(The boulders are) half a meter or so.
Cobbles (cover) about 5 percent of the surface.
Looks like a lot of secondaries though.
The boulder population is really concentrated around the secondaries; and we'll get some pictures of that.
The regolith up here is identical (to that around the LM).
You can see these little lineations which is, I think, a function of Sun angle.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think the boulder population is starting to thin, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: They're getting smaller, and the cobbles are getting smaller.
Looks like we could be just out of this ray.
We don't see any - maybe one or two - of the half-a-meter-size boulders now, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There are a couple of those mounds mapped about 200 meters off to your left.
We were wondering if you could see those.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I got 179 - 180 now - at 0.3; and really on top of a ridge here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Better go over this way more, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: There's North Ray right up there.
Look at the big rocks (on the rim of North Ray), John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You got a good view of North Ray here, and as I look at it, there's a northeast-southwest line of boulders that come out from the southwest rim and go up - (correcting himself) the northeast rim - Smoky Mountain.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We look like we're going into a big sag-type area.
It's at 12 o'clock and 300 or 400 meters, and we're now at
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We may be into Palmetto right now, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's over there on the rim, isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You should be about halfway to Palmetto.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: You're looking (to be) right below (meaning "a bit south of")
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Any resemblance between this and the topo is going to the devil.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay; we're going downslope now, Tony.
(It's) about a 5-degree slope, and we're going to go down perhaps 50 or 60 meters before we start climbing back out again towards Palmetto.
(Pause) And up around North Ray, we see two tremendous blocks at about Station 11 and 12 that appear to be black in color.
And we're just about out of the ray material now.
We only see a few cobbles left.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It might be a good idea to try to get there (near the large boulder) for our Station 11.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: That's what I thought we were going to pick: those two big - big rocks.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It's right up on that ridge.
(Pause) That might be...That's Palmetto right there, I guess, off to the left there, isn't it, John?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The east side is a very shallow slope into this pit.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: How far are we supposed to go this way before we turn back?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You should be heading of about 356.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Lost under Tony) turn...(Stops to listen) Yeah.
Now...At 0.8, you can turn to about 356.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tony, this big depression off to the left that I was describing (meaning the 'big sag' he mentioned at 166:12:40)...on the east side it's a very shallow slope into it, about 4 or 5 degrees.
But, on the far end, the west side and the southwest side, it has very steep walls, 20 degrees or so
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You're looking right at the base of Turtle Mountain.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Lost under Tony) piece of cake, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Laughs) I hate to tell you this, but these mountains don't look the same (as they did in the training model).
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It's off to the left, way off to the left.
We could do a 360 and get a pan of it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: How about that rock there, Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And it's got some lineations in it, huh?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Look at the size of it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Oh, this big one coming up, you mean?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, Tony, it seems to me this is a more subdued (meaning "smoother") surface over here than going towards South Ray.
It's almost...Except for three or four meter-sized craters, it's all subdued and just hummocky and rolling.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Could we have an Amp reading?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Hey, there's about a 4-meter boulder off to our...With a good fillet...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's been there for a while.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: How are we doing now on the rounded versus the angular boulders?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And we can see into Ravine...(Stops to listen) Okay; most of them over here are, I'd say, probably a good half and half.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There are some small, indurated secondary craters.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We've got to...I think it is, John.
Yeah, right up there to the left.
We've been...I think that's 1.1, and we're not quite there yet.
We've got to go on this heading for...(Pause) Okay, Tony, as you look to the northeast, you get a spectacular terrain view of rolling hills occasionally pock-marked with large boulders.
The craters are very subdued, and the hills almost appear smooth, off to the northeast.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...That might be a function of the Sun angle, though.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...according to our track, you're a little bit east of - correction, west - of course, and probably a 005 heading (5 degrees east of north) would take you right along the rim of Palmetto.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I think we're coming up on the rim now, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tony, we topped out on the rim of Palmetto, and hit it right on the nose at
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The walls to the south...south...northeast - correction, northwest - south...Wow!
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I got to keep my eye on the driving.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And to the southeast here are steeper than the walls to the northeast.
Apparently, it looks like it's almost breached to the northeast.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: John's cutting away from the rim now, because it's a little bit easier going.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There's a good ejecta blanket of half-meter-size boulders around the rim of Palmetto into some of these secondary craters here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Do you have an estimate of the coverage (meaning the percentage of the surface covered by rocks)?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (To Houston) Palmetto is as big as Meteor (Crater in Arizona), isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay; of cobble size - in my usual size of being cobbles - I'd say 30 (to) 40 percent of the surface (is covered).
Let's make it 30 percent; and the half-meter size, (there is) maybe one for every 10 square meters.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're just traveling right around the...We're traveling about 100 meters inside the rim, and we're at
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay; and to the northeast, Tony - northwest, correction - you can see large blocks on the rim on the...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hanging right in there, right on the rim.
You won't be able to see the...You wouldn't be...Yeah, you'll be able to see into Palmetto from there.
Okay; the large boulders over there seem to be 3 or 4 meters (in size and they are) to the northwest (means northeast) on the flank of Palmetto, but I think they came from North Ray.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Apparently, the only thing preserved here is...There's the large blocks out of North Ray, but (we) don't see very many small ones.
I think trafficability is going to be excellent, though it looks like a steep slope climbing (to) that (North Ray) rim, doesn't it?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It's not near as bad as Stone Mountain.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The boulder field out of North Ray does not reach Ravine, Tony.
It stops on the outer flank of Ravine about a tenth of a (Ravine) crater diameter away.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Most of the rocks here (at Palmetto) are rounded.
The types are very difficult to identify as we go by.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Palmetto has a very definite raised rim to it, and we're going to be going off the rim down probably a 5- to 10-degree slope into a valley before we start climbing up to North Ray.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's a real valley, too, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And these valleys in here, Tony, tend to trend towards Big Sag.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And Charlie, could you go ahead and...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Yeah, put it on now, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And I got it pointed off to the right...(correcting himself) or left.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're now in an area, Tony, that's at
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Some of these blocks are angular; they're fractured.
They appear to be grayish in color, dust covered, and most all of them have fillets.
That's End Crater right there, John, just over that rim, there, just to your left.
And, Tony, End Crater is on about a 10- to 12-degree slope, pointed toward North Ray.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And we're traveling due east here for a while to pick up a little smooth ground.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're going down a 5-degree slope here or 10?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're about maybe a half a crater diameter to the northeast of Palmetto, about a 10-degree slope, and the boulder population is about 5 degrees (means 5 percent) here.
And the small cobbles have just about disappeared.
Very smooth regolith, except for these 20- to 30-centimeter boulders, which are not very numerous.
We're really moving out, downslope, at about 12, 15 kilometers an hour.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It's remarkable how subdued all these craters are.
It's almost a smooth plain except for a few of the 5-meter craters or so.
The 1-meter size, and all, and smaller, are just about gone.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let's get a better heading here from 2.2 to (means "and") 195, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You should...What they want is about...Just directly north, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It looks great to me, that heading.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It looks like...Well, we're down to about where the rock population is almost nonexistent.
I hope it stays that way for a while.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Y'all are making some outstanding time there.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Hears Tony) Well, he's got it full blower at 11 clicks, and we're just going over an undulating terrain.
The ridge lines here predominantly trend east-west, and they are about 5 meters in (vertical) relief.
And really, the only significant craters that you have out here are the ones that are 5 meters and larger, and they only maybe cover 30 percent of the surface.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Look at those boulders (on North Ray)!
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tony, there are some tremendous boulders on North Ray, and they get bigger as we go nearer them.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tell you one reason why North Ray looks like in the photos, it had such steep walls on one side, is because the rim is raised on one side higher than the other.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Do you think you'll be able to recognize the edge of the continuous ejecta blanket?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Hey, John, it looks like...(Stops to listen) Well, we'll give you a try at that.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think we're starting to get into it right now, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Well, the cobbles and boulders are picking up.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: John, I think it looks like those...See that white boulder dead ahead?
Looks like the greatest variety of boulders is going to be over there.
But that's farther east and our Station 11, or farther north than Station 11 is called for.
It's almost at the foot of Smoky.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Garbled) Let's go up on the rim and see what we've got up here?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay; and you may get a caution flag on Battery 2 temperature...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Tony, in this area now for
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You might watch for a change in soil...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: The Battery 2 temperature is reading what?
You can read that, can't you, Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...color, or albedo as you go along there (as an indication that they are on the North Ray ejecta blanket).
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: That's what we're watching for, and the real change comes up...Oh, man.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (We got) a little closer to the...That was...(Laughs)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's one of those sharp craters...They map sharp out here in the plain.
John, I don't think we're going to be...Go straight between those two big rocks.
I think we're going to have to...Looks like to me that's a pretty steep slope.
If we swing a little bit east here, and then go up just on the edge of that boulder ray right there, we'll make it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tony, now that we get over here and can see down off the flank of North Ray, we can see good boulder rays out of North Ray that go for perhaps...I'm going to say a half-a-crater diameter.
Boulders greater than a meter size.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Could you take a look up at Smoky area there, and see what kind of structure and texture you can see on the face?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Can't see anything except for a couple of rays of boulders out of North Ray that trend...One goes almost into Ravine that I described, and one goes on up to the top (of Smoky Mountain).
In the northeast wall of Ravine, you can see the lineation.
To the north, they are dipping east about 30 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Can you push your camera up that far to get a picture of that?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I don't want to break my RCU bracket; I don't think I can...Wait a minute, I'll take the camera off and do it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Chuckling) Take a picture of that crater the road had us going through.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tony, there's a...That, to me, looks like just a big sink feature, John.
Tony, the road had us...the map had us going...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're definitely in the regolith (meaning the North Ray ejecta) right now, Houston, because, see how these blocks are all laid in there?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Remember how it was up at that crater?
At Schooner (which is a nuclear explosion crater at the Nevada Test Site).
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Those rocks are laid into the ejecta blanket.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: If you didn't know better, you'd say that they were bedrock outcrop, but they're just laid in there, I'm sure, from North Ray.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And as we go to the southeast side of North Ray, there is a big sink feature, a big pit, that's elongate east-west, and we could drive in it from the east.
But once you get to the south of South Ray (means North Ray), it is really a deep pit, Tony.
And that ridge line that we saw from the LM is on the west side of that deep pit.
It's probably 100 meters below the rim of North Ray.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And on the boulders you are looking at now that you think might be thrown in, you might talk about the fillet size away and towards the crater and see if that corresponds with the secondary.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: We're not close to any of them right now.
We're in a very smooth area.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: About a crater diameter from North Ray off to the east, I see some 3-meter boulders that are all rounded and sitting in the ejecta with - or, in the regolith - with good fillets.
Now here's one, Tony, off to the right...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: My bag fell off again, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's a 2-meter-size boulder with a fillet that looks like sort of equi-dimensional around the boulder.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...Equal height (on all sides), I should say.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Do you see any clasts in these boulders?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: We're just passed another 1 meter...(stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (To Tony) They just look whitish to me.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: But that was another one we just passed, 1-meter size, that had the biggest fillet upslope (meaning toward North Ray).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Here's a new crater right there, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tony, these craters that we call secondary, that are indurated, I frankly think are very, very fresh craters, because it looks very cloddy around them, and the other ones that are secondaries do not appear that way.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yeah, I'm not sure that this isn't such an old crater, that the secondaries aren't eroded down.
We've really got good going right here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Now, before we get too far along, let's study this thing and see if we can figure out a way to get up that rim without going through all the boulders in the world...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: See that big one off to the right over there?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think up that slope looks to me to be the best.
Of course, it might be straight ahead might be best.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, I don't see any rocks straight ahead.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tony, we're heading about 300 (60 degrees west of north) and (the bearing to the LM is) 187.
The large boulders, Tony, will be off to our right.
There's a black to brownish looking one, and then there is a solid white one that's right at the base of Smoky Mountain and North Ray.
That might be worth a little jog over there if it's not too far.
It's the most unique white boulder we've seen.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That is a beauty, isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...We'll keep that in mind on the way back.
Give us bearing and range again.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: See that white one over there, John?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Laughing) Man, that is a big rock!
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: They're are not any house-size rocks, but the biggest ones are maybe 5 meters.
(Pause) And it (meaning the surface they are driving on)'s really smooth except for these big rocks out here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There's a real fresh little crater right there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, could you use a couple more words...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It's got a hackly surface to it...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; we're going up a pretty steep slope right now, Houston.
We're doing...I think we're almost at the rim, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Looks like we're just about 20 meters from the rim.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'm going to slow down here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: How about hooking a right, over here, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We got you at about 400 or 500 meters from it yet.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Tony, coming to the rim...(Stops to listen) I don't believe it, but...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...to go to 12 frames per second (on the DAC).
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: This is going to make some great pictures.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Pause) We're on a relatively flat surface now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The rocks here, Tony, are all rounded.
Seventy percent of them are rounded, and the other ones are subangular (meaning that the sharp edges and corners have been partly eroded), mostly dust covered, grayish in color.
The big rocks are not on the rim, Tony!
The big rocks are farther away from the rim.
At least, we can't see any big rocks as we approach the rim, but we're still climbing upslope.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There's a 10-meter boulder off to the right over there, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There's a fresh crater, really fresh one, that has a white interior that's punched in about 2 meters deep, and that was at
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; it looks like to me we're...The rim...Hey, there's some beautiful white ones over there...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Think this is the rim, right here?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We still think you're about 500 meters from the rim.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: We'll be able to sample these white ones.
Here's some...(Stops to listen) We are.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We just climbed...What we thought was the rim of one of these little hummocks.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It was a pretty steep hummock.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...Just like mountain climbing, there's always another ridge.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I'm going to pan the DAC around to get to that boulder field that goes up to Smoky Mountain.
The boulders are very angular over there.
They're dark gray in color, and some of them are almost solid white.
The most unique ones appear to be solid white.
Up on the rim here, they appear to be almost white; none of the dark ones.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I bet it's over there to the left where those rocks are.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We think the most direct route from where you are...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay; we're at one-eighty at four point...(Stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...to the rim would be about a heading of 350.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And there's some beautiful...Those white rocks are...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Can you see on around to see if there are any black rocks around at 3 o'clock in the crater?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Well, we can't see in the crater.
But around at the 3 o'clock position, yeah, there's a biggie.
The biggest one (which is House Rock), Tony, is this 10- to 15-meter boulder that is on the rim; and it's blackish.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Is there any chance of working around towards that contact?
And if we could get both the white and black in one stop, that would be really fine.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I think we can do it with a short stop over there.
And when we get up there, Tony, we might be able to find a black rock.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're going through a...We're definitely on the ejecta blanket here.
And, oh, within 100 meters or so, I think, of the rim.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We have you about 100 meters from it...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Hearing Tony) Man, you guys are right on.
If we copied, I think y'all are right on.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think we can get over there and maybe get them a picture.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That is...That big...I can't believe the size of that big black rock over here.
And I don't think that's a breccia, John, because...Although it might be.
I see some large white clasts.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, the DAC should be out of film; you can turn it off.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Pause) What I'd like to do is park where it's flat, and...(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay; where we came up over here, John, it won't be quite...They get a better view.
Right here's where they get a great view of the interior (of North Ray).
Of the upper third of the wall.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If we go 360 and park right here, it'll be flat.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We can't wait for you to either.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: What was that temperature on Battery 1 again?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Primary (Drive Power) is going to Off.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And, Charlie, we'll need a (Hasselblad) frame count.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Pan...(Stops to listen) Okay; stand by.
I'm so dusty (I can't read the counter).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, I wanted to get in the Sun so you could read it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Took 165 pictures coming up here, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; and we concur on the changeout.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I'm going to put another black and white on.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Man, look at...(Stops to listen) Okay, I'm walking down about a...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: John, I'll get the TV for you.
